sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 14, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 13 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern! Let me analyze the transitions:
1. Transition 1 (UP): A new charcoal pixel appeared at [[63, 63]] - this is the HUD bar growing
2. Transition 3 (UP): The third charcoal target [[36, 32], ...] disappeared! But the blue inside it was still there
3. Transition 4 (UP): Charcoal HUD bar grew to [[63, 62], [63, 63]]
4. Transition 6 (UP): The blue target at [[37, 33], ...] moved to [[33, 33], ...] (up 4 rows)
5. Transition 7 (SPACE): Charcoal HUD bar grew to [[63, 61], [63, 63]]
6. Transition 9 (LEFT): A new charcoal target appeared at [[32, 32], [32, 35], [35, 35], [35, 32]] - the frame reappeared around the blue
7. Transition 10 (SPACE): Charcoal HUD bar grew to [[63, 60], [63, 63]]
So the pattern is:
- Each action causes the charcoal HUD bar at the bottom to grow by 1 pixel
- When UP was pressed (transition 3), the charcoal frame around the blue target disappeared
- When UP was pressed again (transition 6), the blue target moved up 4 rows
- When LEFT was pressed (transition 9), the charcoal frame reappeared around the blue target
Wait, let me reconsider. The key insight is:
- Transition 3 (UP): The charcoal frame at [[36, 32], ...] disappeared. This is the frame that was originally below the player.
- Transition 6 (UP): The blue at [[37, 33], ...] moved to [[33, 33], ...] (up 4 rows)
- Transition 9 (LEFT): A new charcoal frame appeared at [[32, 32], ...] around the now-moved blue
So it seems like:
1. When the player moves UP, the charcoal frame below the player disappears
2. The blue target inside moves up
3. When the player moves LEFT/RIGHT/DOWN, a new charcoal frame appears around the blue target at its new position
Wait, that doesn't quite make sense either. Let me think again...
Actually, I think what's happening is:
- The charcoal frames are "containers" for the blue targets
- When the player moves in a direction, the container in that direction gets "pushed" or "moved"
- The HUD bar at the bottom is counting actions
Let me reconsider. The third target was at [[36, 32], ...] initially. After UP (transition 3), the charcoal frame disappeared. After another UP (transition 6), the blue target moved up 4 rows to [[33, 33], ...]. After LEFT (transition 9), a new charcoal frame appeared at [[32, 32], ...] which is around the moved blue target.
So the mechanic might be:
- Moving UP when there's a target below the player "releases" the blue from its charcoal frame
- The blue then moves up
- Moving in other directions causes a new charcoal frame to appear around the blue
Or maybe:
- The player can "pick up" blue targets by moving in their direction
- The blue target moves with the player or to a new position
- The charcoal frame is a "holder" that gets removed when the blue is picked up
Let me think about what the goal might be. Maybe the goal is to collect all three blue targets by moving in their direction? The charcoal frames are holders, and when you move toward a holder, the blue gets released and moves.
Let me try: move UP again from current position. The player is at rows 40-42, cols 28-31. The blue target that was released is at [[33, 33], ...]. If I move UP, maybe the blue will move further up or get collected.
Actually wait - let me look at the transition 3 more carefully. The charcoal frame at [[36, 32], ...] disappeared. This was the frame that was directly below the player's starting position (player was at rows 49-51, cols 32-35, frame was at rows 36-39, cols 32-35). So the frame was above the player, not below.
Let me reconsider the layout:
- Player start: rows 49-51, cols 32-35
- Third target (charcoal frame): rows 36-39, cols 32-35 (above player, same columns)
- Blue inside third target: rows 37-38, cols 33-34
So when the player pressed UP, the charcoal frame above the player disappeared. Then on the next UP, the blue moved up. Then on LEFT, a new frame appeared around the blue.
I think the mechanic is:
1. Moving toward a target (in the direction of the target) "breaks" the charcoal frame
2. The blue target then moves in that direction
3. Moving away causes a new frame to appear
Or maybe:
1. Moving UP "pulls" the target above toward the player
2. The charcoal frame breaks when the target is pulled
3. The blue moves toward the player
Let me try pressing UP again from the current position to see what happens. The player is at rows 40-42, cols 28-31. The blue target is at [[33, 33], ...] (rows 33-34, cols 33-34). If I move UP, the player would go to rows 37-39 (approximately), which is still below the blue target.
Actually, let me reconsider the movement amounts:
- RIGHT: +4 cols
- LEFT: -3 cols (but this seems inconsistent)
- UP: -3 or -4 rows (blocked sometimes)
- DOWN: +4 rows
Let me try moving UP now and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key findings from transition analysis:
# - Each action grows the charcoal HUD bar at bottom-right by 1 pixel
# - Transition 3 (UP): Charcoal frame at [[36,32]...] disappeared
# - Transition 6 (UP): Blue target moved from [[37,33]...] to [[33,33]...] (up 4 rows)
# - Transition 9 (LEFT): New charcoal frame appeared at [[32,32]...] around the moved blue

# The pattern: UP breaks the charcoal frame, blue moves up, LEFT/RIGHT/DOWN creates new frame
# This means UP is the "collect" action for targets above

# Current state:
# - Player at rows 40-42, cols 28-31
# - Blue target (released) at [[33,33]...], with charcoal frame at [[32,32]...]
# - Two other targets still intact: [[24,44]...] and [[28,16]...]

# Try moving UP to see if it collects the blue target
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves player +4 cols, creates new charcoal frame around released blue", "UP": "Moves player up, breaks charcoal frames, releases blue targets upward", "LEFT": "Moves player -3 cols, creates new charcoal frame around released blue", "DOWN": "Moves player +4 rows", "SPACE": "Grows HUD bar but no gameplay effect"}, "object_taxonomy": {"charcoal_frame": "Holder for blue targets, breaks when player moves UP toward it", "blue_center": "Target to collect, moves when frame breaks", "green_object": "Player", "white_bar": "Indicator, follows player", "pink_bar": "HUD timer", "charcoal_hud_bar": "Bottom-right corner, grows with each action"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "pink bar + charcoal counter, HUD"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1: UP breaks charcoal frames and releases blue targets upward. Other directions re-create frames.", "goal_model": "Collect all 3 blue targets by moving UP toward them", "recent_findings": ["UP breaks charcoal frames", "Blue moves up when frame breaks", "Other directions re-create frames"], "open_questions": ["Does repeated UP collect the blue?"], "current_plan": "Move UP to collect the released blue target", "strategy_log": ["UP is the key mechanic"], "failed_probes": ["SPACE to collect"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
